statusLine: EU fuel stocks remain under pressure with 16 of 27 countries holding critical reserves in at least one fuel type, despite overall EU averages above mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant strain as oil prices surge 45.1% since late February to $105.19/barrel, forcing widespread drawdowns of strategic reserves. While EU-wide averages for petrol (113 days), diesel (102.2 days), and jet fuel (96.4 days) remain above the 90-day mandatory minimum, the distribution reveals concerning vulnerabilities across member states.

Romania presents the most severe situation with all three fuel types in critical status - petrol at just 40.4 days, diesel at 30.2 days, and jet fuel at 76.4 days. Poland follows with similarly dire conditions across all categories. Luxembourg, despite its small size, also shows critical levels in all fuel types. Meanwhile, 16 of 27 EU countries hold critical reserves in at least one fuel category, indicating widespread pressure on national stockpiles.

Jet fuel emerges as the most constrained fuel type, with 17 countries holding critical or warning-level reserves. This reflects both aviation demand recovery and supply chain disruptions from Middle East tensions. Diesel shortages affect 12 countries at critical levels, while petrol shows the most stability with only 7 countries in critical status. The stark price variations - from Malta's €1.34/L to Netherlands' €2.388/L for petrol - highlight the uneven impact across the union.
keyPoints: 16 of 27 EU countries hold critical fuel reserves in at least one category despite overall EU averages above mandatory minimums
Romania faces the most severe shortages with all fuel types critical: petrol 40.4 days, diesel 30.2 days, jet fuel 76.4 days
Jet fuel shows greatest vulnerability with 17 countries at critical or warning levels, reflecting aviation sector strain
Oil prices surged 45.1% since late February to $105.19/barrel, driving strategic reserve releases across multiple countries
Diesel remains tight in 12 countries at critical levels, while petrol shows relatively better stability with 7 critical countries
